text: The surgeon closes the fascial defect after performing lysis of adhesions and reducing the hernia contents, ensuring the tissue layers are securely approximated.

The surgeon creates small stab incisions along the midline over the hernia defect and inserts a disposable suture passer device. The device traverses the subcutaneous tissue and pierces the fascia edge laterally on one side. A grasper transfers the suture intra-abdominally, and the suture passer is reinserted through the same incision to pass through the fascia on the opposite side.

The surgeon places sutures one centimeter apart along the midline fascial edges. After every four sutures, the abdomen is desufflated, and the sutures are tied extracorporeally to approximate the fascial edges. This process is repeated until the sutures extend slightly beyond the cephalad and caudal ends of the hernia.

Twelve sero-PDF sutures are placed along the hernia defect, followed by securing the mesh with cardinal sero-PDF sutures at its four corners. Permanent tags are then applied using a double crown technique to ensure stability.
